山吹

Yamabuki — a SNES emulator in Zig, and how its bugs were actually found

Yamabuki is a SNES emulator written in Zig, built to run at full speed on cheap ARM handhelds.

⟨CLICK⟩

Three parts. First, what it is — the emulator, the games, the CRT shaders. Then how it's built. And then the part I actually care about: how the bugs were found, which turned out to be nothing like where the tests were looking.

SNES emulator · Zig · ARM handhelds

Three parts: what · how built · how found

A SNES, in about twenty thousand lines

  • Runs full speed on a handheld — a Cortex-A53 with no cycles to spare is the design target, not an afterthought
  • Accuracy on demand — a cycle-exact core you can switch on per game, built from the same source
  • Save states, anywhere — and a libretro core, so it drops into RetroArch
  • Twelve CRT shaders — the good ones, compiled ahead of time, cycled with , and .
  • Nothing to install — one binary, no dependencies, static on the handheld

It's a Super Nintendo, in about twenty thousand lines of Zig.

⟨CLICK⟩

The target is a cheap ARM handheld — a Cortex-A53 class chip with nothing spare. That's a design constraint from day one, not something bolted on later.

⟨CLICK⟩

There's a cycle-exact core you can turn on per game when you need it, and — this is the interesting bit — it's built from the same source as the fast one.

⟨CLICK⟩

Save states anywhere, and a libretro core so it drops straight into RetroArch.

⟨CLICK⟩

Twelve CRT shaders, compiled ahead of time — we'll come back to that.

⟨CLICK⟩

And nothing to install. One binary. No dependencies at all.

Handheld is the target, not an afterthought

Accurate core, same source

Save states · libretro

12 shaders, baked

One binary, zero deps

The games

It runs the actual library.

And it runs the actual library. Super Metroid, Turtles in Time, Donkey Kong Country, EarthBound, Mega Man X, Final Fantasy the Sixth, Super Mario Kart — all of these are running in Yamabuki, rendered through crt-royale on a desktop GPU.

⟨CLICK⟩

Hold that thought, though. These are the first commercial games this emulator has ever run — and the day we pointed it at them, it told us some very uncomfortable things. That's the third part of this talk.

The real library, through crt-royale

First commercial games it ever ran → part 3

Shaders: making it look like a television

A SNES was never seen on a flat panel. It was seen through phosphors, scanlines, and a slightly curved piece of glass.

  • Twelve presets from the libretro collection — crt-royale, crt-guest-advanced, crt-lottes and the rest
  • , and . cycle through them live, without restarting
  • Every one is compiled on the build machine, never on the handheld

Drag the divider on the next slides. Or just click the picture — it flips.

Now: shaders. Nobody ever saw a Super Nintendo on a flat panel. They saw it through a cathode ray tube — phosphors, scanlines, a slightly curved piece of glass, and a lot of glow.

⟨CLICK⟩

So Yamabuki ships twelve of the best CRT shaders from the libretro collection.

⟨CLICK⟩

Comma and full stop cycle through them live.

⟨CLICK⟩

And every one of them is compiled on the build machine, never on the device — which is a whole story of its own, in part two.

⟨CLICK⟩

The next few slides are live. Drag the divider to wipe between the raw console output and the shader. Or just click the picture and it flips all the way over.

A SNES was never seen on a flat panel

12 libretro presets · ',' '.' to cycle

Compiled on the build host

NEXT: drag the divider, or click to flip

Handheld tier

Cheap enough for a Cortex-A53

These four are the handheld tier — single pass, cheap, built for weak fill rate. Same game, same frame, so the shader is the only thing changing.

⟨CLICK⟩

Drag any of them. On the left is exactly what the console puts out — hard pixels, no filtering. On the right is what you'd actually be looking at.

⟨CLICK⟩

zfast-crt is the cheapest thing that still reads as a CRT. crt-pi was written for a Raspberry Pi. crt-lottes-fast is Timothy Lottes' shader with the expensive parts taken out. And crt-easymode is the one most people settle on.

Handheld tier — single pass, cheap

Same game, same frame — shader is the only variable

DRAG one. Left = raw console output

Desktop tier

And these want a real GPU

The desktop tier. Multi-pass, mask lookup tables, real fill rate. I picked Castlevania the Fourth on purpose — it's dark, and a dark scene is where a CRT shader earns its keep.

⟨CLICK⟩

Watch the purple mist and the glow around the light sources as you drag. That's halation — light bleeding sideways inside the tube. You cannot fake it with a scanline overlay, and it is the single biggest reason these games looked better in 1993 than they do on your monitor.

Desktop tier — multi-pass, mask LUTs

Dark scene on purpose

Watch the glow — halation, not scanlines

The heavyweight

…and one that isn't a CRT at all

Two very different answers to the same question.

⟨CLICK⟩

On the left, crt-royale — the most elaborate CRT simulation anyone has written. It models the phosphor mask, the beam profile, the halation, the glass. It is enormously expensive and it is worth every cycle.

⟨CLICK⟩

On the right, sharp-bilinear, which isn't a CRT shader at all. It just scales the pixels without making them blurry. Some people don't want a television — they want the pixels, clean. That's a legitimate taste and it ships too.

crt-royale — models mask, beam, halation, glass

sharp-bilinear — not a CRT at all, just clean pixels

Both are legitimate. Both ship.

crt-royale, close up

One more, big enough that you can actually see what's happening. Chrono Trigger, through crt-royale.

⟨CLICK⟩

Drag it slowly. Look at the clouds — on the raw side they're flat bands of colour with hard steps between them. Through the tube they blend, because the phosphors bleed into each other and your eye does the rest. That dithering pattern the artist used was designed to be blurred. On a flat panel it's a visible checkerboard. On a CRT it's a gradient.

⟨CLICK⟩

This is the whole argument for CRT shaders in one picture. The artists were drawing for a display we stopped using.

Chrono Trigger · crt-royale, large

The clouds: flat bands → gradient

The dithering WAS designed to be blurred

Artists drew for a display we stopped using

Honesty

The tier is a promise, not a rating.

crt-royale on a Mali-G31 is a slideshow — and the package says so, rather than letting you find out.

Every preset is tagged handheld or desktop, and the tag is printed at startup. It is a claim about a specific class of device, not a star rating.

One thing about how these are shipped, because it's the same idea that runs through the whole project.

⟨CLICK⟩

Each shader is tagged handheld or desktop, and the tag is printed when it loads. That tag is not a quality rating. It's a promise about a specific class of device. crt-royale on a Mali-G31 is a slideshow — and the package tells you that, rather than letting you discover it yourself on a bus.

Tier = a promise about a device class

Not a star rating

Say it up front, don't let them find out

The numbers

What the project is, before what it argues

19,907lines of Zig
5.12MCPU test cases, cycle-exact — count and bus position
100golden framebuffer & audio hashes
203unit tests
0build-time dependencies

Part two: how it's built. Some numbers first, so you know the shape of the thing.

⟨CLICK⟩

About twenty thousand lines of Zig.

⟨CLICK⟩

The 65816 CPU is held to full cycle parity against five point one two million test vectors — and not just the right answer at the end, the right bus access on the right cycle.

⟨CLICK⟩

A hundred golden hashes lock the renderer and the audio.

⟨CLICK⟩

Two hundred and three unit tests.

⟨CLICK⟩

And zero build-time dependencies. That last zero is load-bearing — half the decisions in this project exist to protect it. Remember that number, because at the end of part three I'm going to tell you that almost none of this caught the bugs that mattered.

~20k lines

5.12M cases — cycle-exact

100 goldens · 203 unit tests

Zero deps — load-bearing

TEASE: none of it caught the real bugs

Performance is gated deterministically

Not by wall clock — by counters that cannot drift

.{ .path = "Rings.sfc",
   .steps      =  1582245,   // instructions retired
   .cycles     = 42884192,   // master cycles elapsed
   .vram_reads = 14672028 }  // renderer VRAM word fetches

// delete the tile-row decode cache -> vram_reads goes up ~8x

// CI goes red. Not "slower on this runner". Red.

Performance regressions are caught by counters, not by a stopwatch.

⟨CLICK⟩

Every benchmark ROM pins three numbers: instructions retired, master cycles elapsed, and how many words the renderer fetched from video RAM.

⟨CLICK⟩

If you delete the tile decode cache, VRAM traffic goes up roughly eightfold.

⟨CLICK⟩

And CI goes red. Not "hmm, this run looks a bit slow" — red, deterministically, on any machine, including a laptop on battery. A wall-clock benchmark in CI is a coin flip you run on every commit.

Counters, not a stopwatch

steps · cycles · vram_reads

Delete the cache → 8x traffic → RED

Wall-clock CI = a coin flip

Accuracy is a comptime parameter

  • Console(cfg) is instantiated three times — fast, accurate, and profiling
  • Only seven sites in the whole core branch on the accuracy flag, and they resolve at compile time
  • The shipped emulator carries no branch at all for the other two
  • Runtime selection is a tagged union, dispatched once per frame — never in a hot loop

The accurate core and the fast core are the same source file.

⟨CLICK⟩

Console is a function of a compile-time config, instantiated three times now — the fast core, the cycle-exact core, and a third one we'll meet in part three.

⟨CLICK⟩

Only seven places in the entire core branch on the accuracy flag, and every one of them is resolved by the compiler.

⟨CLICK⟩

Which means the emulator you ship carries no branch, no flag, and no cost for the modes you're not using. They're different types.

⟨CLICK⟩

The only runtime indirection is one tagged union, dispatched once per frame.

Console(cfg) × 3 — fast, accurate, profiling

7 branch sites, all comptime

Shipped core: zero cost for modes you don't use

A pointer is a compile error

  • Save states are generated by comptime reflection over the machine's fields
  • The serializer refuses to compile if it meets a pointer — derived state must be rebuilt, not stored
  • postLoad() rebuilds the page table and the caches
  • byteSize(T) is comptime-known, so libretro's retro_serialize_size is honest and stable

Save states aren't written by hand. They're generated at compile time by walking the machine's own fields.

⟨CLICK⟩

And the serializer refuses to compile if it ever meets a pointer.

⟨CLICK⟩

That's the interesting part. A pointer in a save state is always a bug — you'd be writing an address into a file and reading it back into a different process. So instead of documenting "don't do that", it's a type error. Derived state gets rebuilt in postLoad.

⟨CLICK⟩

And because the size is known at compile time, the number libretro asks for is the number it gets. Forever.

Comptime reflection, not hand-written

A pointer WON'T COMPILE

Derived state rebuilt in postLoad()

Honest retro_serialize_size

The exception

The one rule the compiler can't enforce

// The bus page table holds pointers INTO this struct, and the CPU
// holds &self.bus. A Console must be heap-allocated, initialised in
// place, and NEVER MOVED.
//
// A copied Console's page table points at its corpse.
const con = try gpa.create(Console);
con.init(cart);

Documented three times. It is the only invariant in the project that lives in a comment.

Here's the exception, and I want to be honest about it.

⟨CLICK⟩

The Console is self-referential — the bus page table holds pointers into the Console's own memory. So if you copy a Console, its page table points at where it used to live. A corpse.

⟨CLICK⟩

Zig can't stop you from copying a struct. So this is the one rule in the entire project that is enforced by a comment — three comments, actually, because that's the only tool available.

⟨CLICK⟩

Everything else in this deck is about turning a rule into something a machine checks. This slide is here because I couldn't.

Console is self-referential

Copy it → page table points at a corpse

The ONE rule enforced by a comment

Be honest: I couldn't machine-check this one

Correctness is externally anchored

  • Test vectors and test ROMs are fetched and revision-pinned, never vendored — only the hashes are committed
  • 5.12M CPU cases check the bus access on every cycle, not just the final register state
  • Every golden is replayed through three drivers — fast core, accurate core, libretro core

None of the correctness data is mine.

⟨CLICK⟩

The CPU vectors and the test ROMs are fetched from upstream and pinned to a revision. They are never copied into this repository. What's committed is the hashes — so if upstream changes, I find out.

⟨CLICK⟩

And the CPU tests check per-cycle bus behaviour, not just "did you end up with the right value in A".

⟨CLICK⟩

Every golden hash is replayed through all three frontends, because a bug that only appears through libretro is still a bug.

Fetched + pinned, never vendored

Per-cycle bus, not just final state

Every golden × 3 drivers

Zero build-time dependencies is a hard constraint

  • SDL3 is not linked — the ABI is hand-ported and the library is dlopen'd at runtime
  • zig build alone cross-compiles to aarch64-linux-musl. No sysroot, no toolchain, no package manager
  • The handheld package asserts it is static — no interpreter, no NEEDED entries — and fails the build if it isn't

Zero dependencies isn't a boast, it's a constraint that shapes everything.

⟨CLICK⟩

SDL3 isn't linked. The ABI is hand-ported and the library is opened at runtime. If it isn't there, you lose the window — not the emulator.

⟨CLICK⟩

Which means zig build, on its own, cross-compiles for the handheld. No sysroot. No cross toolchain.

⟨CLICK⟩

And the packaging step asserts the result is actually static, and fails if it isn't. Because "we're pretty sure it's static" is how you find out it wasn't, on someone else's device.

SDL3 dlopen'd, not linked

zig build alone → aarch64 musl

Packaging ASSERTS static, or fails

Shaders: compile on the laptop, not the handheld

The emulator contains no shader compiler. None.

  • The presets are Vulkan GLSL. RetroArch links glslang and SPIRV-Cross — about 150,000 lines of C++ — and compiles them on the device
  • Yamabuki runs that pipeline on the build host and ships the result: plain GLSL, plus a manifest of reflected uniform offsets
  • The phosphor mask PNGs are decoded at bake time too — so there's no image decoder either
  • The tools are built by zig c++. Even the bake step needs no toolchain the repo doesn't already pin

Back to the shaders, from the other side.

⟨CLICK⟩

Those presets are written in Vulkan GLSL. The normal way to run them is what RetroArch does: link glslang and SPIRV-Cross — a hundred and fifty thousand lines of C++ — into your binary, and compile the shaders on the device at load time.

⟨CLICK⟩

Yamabuki runs exactly that pipeline, but on the build host, and ships the output. Plain GLSL, and a manifest saying where every uniform lives.

⟨CLICK⟩

The phosphor mask images get decoded at bake time as well, which means the emulator doesn't contain a PNG decoder either.

⟨CLICK⟩

And the tools that do the baking are compiled by zig c-plus-plus. Zig ships clang. So even the bake step needs nothing this repo doesn't already require.

No shader compiler in the binary. None.

RetroArch: 150k lines of C++, compiled ON DEVICE

We bake on the host → plain GLSL + manifest

No PNG decoder either

Tools built by zig c++

What baking buys

  • The design goals survive — pure-Zig core, dependency-free build, static-musl package still passes its own assertion
  • Nothing compiles on the device: an A53 doesn't spend its startup budget on GLSL, and a vendor driver bug surfaces on your laptop
  • Every ambiguity — offsets, samplers, aliases, feedback, LUT dimensions — is resolved once, where it can fail loudly
  • A shader that can't work is absent, not broken. 31 of 36 pairs bake; the 5 skips are printed with their reason

What that buys.

⟨CLICK⟩

The design goals survive — all three of them. A runtime shader compiler would have cost every one.

⟨CLICK⟩

Nothing is compiled on the device, so a Cortex-A53 doesn't spend its startup budget parsing GLSL, and a bug in some vendor's shader compiler shows up on your laptop instead of in someone's hands.

⟨CLICK⟩

Every ambiguity is resolved once, at bake time, where it can fail loudly.

⟨CLICK⟩

And a shader that can't work is absent rather than broken. Thirty-one of thirty-six preset-profile pairs bake; the five that don't are printed with the reason.

⟨CLICK⟩

Hold on to that last line. In part three it turns out not to have been true.

Design goals survive — all three

Nothing compiles on device

Ambiguity resolved once, loudly

"Can't work → absent, not broken"

TEASE: that last one wasn't true

Making it fast is mostly not doing work

  • Tile-row decode cache — decode a whole 8-pixel row once instead of re-reading every plane word per pixel. ~8× less VRAM traffic, +18–39% on background-heavy scenes
  • SA-1 ROM-read fast path — on an SA-1 cart, every fetch of the second CPU went through a switch. It was 38% of total work on Super Mario RPG. Precompute the bank map instead: +15%
  • Both are bit-identical — every golden hash unchanged, which is the only reason they were safe to make

Two examples of making it faster, and they have the same shape: stop doing work you already did.

⟨CLICK⟩

The renderer used to re-read the same planar words out of video RAM for every single pixel. Now it decodes a whole eight-pixel row at once. That's roughly eight times less VRAM traffic, and between eighteen and thirty-nine percent more frames per second on background-heavy scenes.

⟨CLICK⟩

And on an SA-1 cartridge there's a second CPU whose every instruction fetch was going through a switch statement. Profiling said that was thirty-eight percent of total work on Super Mario RPG. Precomputing the bank map instead bought fifteen percent.

⟨CLICK⟩

Both changes are bit-identical — every golden hash unchanged. That's not a nice-to-have. That's the only reason it was safe to touch the renderer at all.

Both = stop redoing work

Tile-row cache: ~8x less VRAM traffic

SA-1 fast path: was 38% of total work → +15%

BIT-IDENTICAL — that's why it was safe

Part three

Every test passed.

5.12 million CPU cases. 100 golden hashes. 203 unit tests. A deterministic performance gate. All green, on every commit.

Now let me tell you what they didn't catch.

So. Part three.

⟨CLICK⟩

Everything in part two was green. Five million CPU cases, a hundred golden hashes, two hundred unit tests, a performance gate that can't drift. Every commit, all green.

⟨CLICK⟩

And now I'm going to tell you what all of that failed to catch — because I think it's the most useful thing in this talk.

All green. Every commit.

Now: what it didn't catch

Reality check

Every golden test in the repo is homebrew.

A hundred of them pass. They are all synthetic test ROMs — written by people testing emulators, for people testing emulators.

Then we pointed it at twenty of the actual SNES library, for the first time.

16 / 20boot and render
2 / 20correctly refuse — PAL carts on an NTSC machine
4 / 20never render a single frame
100 / 100homebrew goldens — still green

Every golden test in this repository is homebrew. A hundred of them pass, and they're all synthetic — ROMs written by emulator people, for emulator people.

⟨CLICK⟩

Then we ran twenty of the canonical library. Sixteen boot. Two refuse on purpose — they're PAL cartridges and this is an NTSC machine, so they correctly print "this game pak is not designed for your Super NES". That's not a bug, that's the game being right.

⟨CLICK⟩

Four never render a frame at all. And the hundred goldens stayed green through every one of them.

All 100 goldens are HOMEBREW

16 boot · 2 correctly refuse (PAL) · 4 dead

The goldens stayed green throughout

What no test could see

Three bugs a GPU found, and a unit test never would

  • The picture was upside down. In every shader, since the first commit. GL's origin is bottom-left. It survived because the only test image was radially symmetric
  • Mipmaps were generated on the wrong texture — leaving the source incomplete, and GL samples an incomplete texture as pure black. crt-royale rendered nothing at all
  • A limit set to 64, and crt-guest-advanced declares 148 parameters. The baker had no such limit, so it happily emitted a manifest the runtime then refused

The shader pipeline was compile-verified on four targets. Every fallible piece of logic — the pass geometry, the manifest parsing, the uniform encoding — was unit-tested. Then we ran it on an actual graphics card.

⟨CLICK⟩

The picture was upside down. In every shader. Since the very first commit. OpenGL's origin is the bottom-left corner and mine was the top. And nobody had ever noticed, because the one test image we ever looked at was radially symmetric. Rotate it a hundred and eighty degrees and it's the same picture.

⟨CLICK⟩

Mipmaps were being generated on each pass's output instead of its input, which left the source texture incomplete — and OpenGL samples an incomplete texture as pure black. So crt-royale, the flagship, rendered absolutely nothing.

⟨CLICK⟩

And a parameter limit was set to sixty-four, while crt-guest-advanced declares a hundred and forty-eight. The baker had no such limit, so it cheerfully produced a file the runtime refused to load.

⟨CLICK⟩

Three bugs. Zero failing tests. The only thing that found them was pointing it at a monitor and looking.

Upside down — since commit ONE

Hidden by a radially symmetric test image

Mipmaps on the wrong texture → black

Limit 64 vs 148 params

Zero failing tests. Looking found them.

And it happened again

Ten of the twelve shaders work.

While making the slides for this deck, two of them — crt-geom and crt-hyllian — turned out to render a pure black frame on a real GPU.

They bake. They compile. They link. Every uniform resolves. And they are the only two presets that had never been run.

"A shader that can't work is absent, not broken."

That claim was in part two of this deck. It was not true.

And then it happened again, this week, while I was making the slides you just looked at.

⟨CLICK⟩

Two of the twelve shaders — crt-geom and crt-hyllian — render a pure black frame on a real graphics card. They bake successfully. They compile. They link. Every uniform location resolves and every parameter is uploaded. And they are precisely, exactly, the only two presets that nobody had ever actually run, because the GPU session that found the last three bugs happened to test the other ones.

⟨CLICK⟩

Twenty minutes ago in this deck I told you: a shader that can't work is absent, not broken. I believed that. It's in the README. It was not true, and the only reason I know is that I tried to take a screenshot of it.

Found THIS WEEK, making these slides

crt-geom + crt-hyllian → pure black

Bake, compile, link, uniforms resolve

The only 2 nobody ever RAN

Callback: "absent, not broken" — it wasn't

You cannot measure "is this game too slow"

Not directly. The SNES CPU burns exactly the same cycles every frame — the scheduler runs it to the scanline's clock target, always. It never overruns its budget. It never gets the chance.

When a game is too slow, its main loop simply fails to come round before the next vblank, and a frame is dropped.

So measure the complement: the time the CPU spends waiting.

Different bug, same lesson. This one starts with a question: is a given game short of CPU? It matters, because there's a community that hand-converts SNES games to run on the SA-1 co-processor, and each conversion is weeks of reverse engineering. You'd like to know if it's worth it.

⟨CLICK⟩

And you cannot measure it. Not directly. On a SNES the CPU burns exactly the same number of cycles every single frame — the scheduler runs it to the scanline target, always. It never overruns its budget, because it never gets the chance to.

⟨CLICK⟩

What actually happens when a game is too slow is that its main loop doesn't come round before the next vblank, and a frame gets dropped.

⟨CLICK⟩

So you measure the opposite thing. Not the time it spent working — the time it spent waiting. Idle time is headroom, and headroom is exactly what a faster chip would buy back.

Q: is this game short of CPU?

SNES CPU burns the SAME cycles every frame

It never overruns — it never gets the chance

Measure the COMPLEMENT: waiting

A loop is a wait if it goes nowhere

wait:  LDA $10          ; a flag the NMI handler sets
       BEQ wait

wait:  LDA $4212        ; ...or poll the hardware directly
       BPL wait

sum:   LDA $2000,y      ; and this one is a CHECKSUM
       ADC $04          ; tight, repetitive, writes nothing
       INY              ; for four thousand iterations
       CPY #$1000       ; and it is *working*
       BNE sum

The tell is that the checksum walks. A wait watches one address forever — because watching one spot for something else to change it is what waiting is.

So: find the waiting. And this is where it gets hard, because a loop that's waiting and a loop that's working look almost identical.

⟨CLICK⟩

Here's a wait. Read a flag, branch back.

⟨CLICK⟩

Here's another wait — same shape, but polling a hardware register instead.

⟨CLICK⟩

And here's a checksum. Tight, repetitive, writes nothing for four thousand iterations — and it is doing real work.

⟨CLICK⟩

The difference is that the checksum walks. Every pass it reads a different address. A wait reads the same one, forever, because watching one spot for something else to change it is what waiting actually is.

Waiting and working look IDENTICAL

Two waits, one checksum

The checksum WALKS. A wait watches one spot.

The actual lesson

I got that rule wrong four times.

And every single correction was forced by a game, not by thinking harder.

Contra IIIIts wait is a JSL inside a BRA loop — seven addresses spread over 6 KiB. I was bounding the span of the program counter. It read 100% busy on every frame, title screen included
the stackThat same JSL pushes three bytes every pass — which my "a wait writes nothing" test counted as a write. A call and its return leave the machine exactly as they found it
Tetris & Dr. MarioIts wait stirs a random seed while it spins — that's how the game seeds randomness from how long you took to press Start. So a wait is allowed to write
Super Mario WorldA memory clear that merely preceded a wait condemned it, because I judged a whole window at once. Its idle time vanished entirely

Now. I got that rule wrong four times, and this is the part I actually want you to take away.

⟨CLICK⟩

Contra III. Its wait loop is a subroutine call inside a branch loop — seven addresses spread over six kilobytes. I was checking whether the program counter stayed in a small range, which throws out every subroutine-shaped wait ever written. Contra III came out at a hundred percent CPU utilisation on every frame — including its title screen. That's what gave it away.

⟨CLICK⟩

Then that same call pushes three bytes onto the stack every time round, and my "a wait writes nothing" test counted that as a write. But a call and its return leave the machine exactly as they found it. Stack traffic isn't a side effect.

⟨CLICK⟩

Then Tetris and Doctor Mario. Its wait loop stirs a random number seed while it spins — which is how the game seeds randomness from how long you took to press Start. So a wait is allowed to write. It just isn't allowed to write its way through a buffer.

⟨CLICK⟩

And Super Mario World, where a memory clear that merely happened to come before a wait condemned it, because I was judging a whole window of instructions at once. Its idle time disappeared completely.

⟨CLICK⟩

Four bugs. Not one of them was found by reasoning about the code. Every single one was a game contradicting me.

FOUR wrong rules. All corrected by GAMES.

Contra III — span vs return. 100% on the title screen.

Stack — a JSL push is not a side effect

Tetris — a wait MAY write (RNG stir)

SMW — judge a pass, not a window

None found by thinking harder.

The dead end is worth keeping

"A wait can't exit on its own — so a loop ended by an interrupt was waiting."

Plausible. Wrong in both halves:

  • A loop polling $4212 exits under its own power the moment the hardware sets the bit. No interrupt required
  • And any long-running loop is eventually interrupted by the vblank NMI — checksums included. It didn't even exclude the case it existed to exclude

It's written down in the source, because a plausible idea that is wrong will be had again.

One idea I had along the way is written into the source code as a dead end, and I think that's worth doing.

⟨CLICK⟩

The idea was: a wait loop can't exit on its own, so if a loop was ended by an interrupt, it was waiting. It sounds right. It's wrong in both halves.

⟨CLICK⟩

A loop polling the hardware register exits by itself the moment the hardware sets the bit. No interrupt needed.

⟨CLICK⟩

And any long-running loop gets interrupted by the vblank NMI eventually — including checksums. So the rule didn't even exclude the thing it was invented to exclude.

⟨CLICK⟩

It's in the source as a comment, because a plausible idea that happens to be wrong is an idea somebody will have again. Probably me.

"Interrupt-terminated = waiting"

Wrong in BOTH halves

$4212 poll exits on its own

Every long loop gets NMI'd — checksums too

Write down the dead ends

Debugging the debugger

When the diagnostic came back empty, the diagnostic was the bug.

A game was reporting 100% busy. I asked the profiler which loops it was spending its time in, and it printed an empty table.

Because the hot-loop table only recorded idle cycles. A game stuck at 100% busy has none — so it showed nothing, at exactly the moment I needed it most.

Here's a small one that I enjoyed.

⟨CLICK⟩

A game was reporting a hundred percent CPU. So I asked the profiler: fine, which loops is it spending all that time in? And it printed an empty table.

⟨CLICK⟩

Because the hot-loop table only recorded loops it had classified as idle. And a game stuck at a hundred percent busy has none of those, by definition. The diagnostic was blind in exactly the situation it existed for.

⟨CLICK⟩

If your instrument shows you nothing, consider that it might be the instrument.

Game says 100% busy. Ask which loops. Empty table.

Table only recorded IDLE cycles

Blind in exactly the case it existed for

If the instrument shows nothing — suspect the instrument

The payoff

0% utilisation doesn't mean idle. It means stuck.

$8616:  BIT $4212      ; HVBJOY
$8619:  BVC $8616      ; loop until V is set

; BIT puts bit 6 of the operand straight into the V flag.
; Bit 6 of HVBJOY is the H-blank flag.
;
; in_hblank was declared. Initialised to false. Read by
; readHvbjoy(). And assigned by NOBODY.

Thirteen carts sat at 0% CPU, never polling the pad. Not slow — waiting, forever, for a bit that was hard-wired to zero.

And then the profiler paid for itself, in a way I did not plan.

⟨CLICK⟩

Thirteen cartridges in the library reported zero percent CPU utilisation and never once read the controller. Now — zero percent isn't a slow game. A slow game is busy. Zero percent means the CPU is doing nothing at all. It's not crashed, it's not lost: it is waiting. Forever.

⟨CLICK⟩

So I asked the profiler where, and it gave me an address, and there were two instructions at it. BIT, then branch-if-overflow-clear. BIT puts bit six of whatever you read straight into the overflow flag — and bit six of that register is the horizontal blank flag. This is how you wait for H-blank.

⟨CLICK⟩

And in Yamabuki, the H-blank flag was declared. Initialised to false. Read by the register handler. And assigned. By. Nobody. It was hard-wired to zero, so that branch looped until the heat death of the universe.

13 carts at 0% CPU, never polling the pad

0% ≠ slow. 0% = STUCK. Waiting forever.

BIT $4212 / BVC → bit 6 = H-blank

in_hblank: declared, initialised, read — ASSIGNED BY NOBODY

Four lines. F-Zero boots.

A LoROM launch title with no coprocessor had never drawn a frame. The hundred golden ROMs never noticed — because all hundred are homebrew, and homebrew doesn't wait for H-blank.

Four lines to derive that flag from the beam position.

⟨CLICK⟩

And F-Zero boots. Title screen, music, and its Mode 7 attract demo. That's a launch title — LoROM, no coprocessor, about as ordinary a cartridge as the SNES has — and it had never drawn a single frame.

⟨CLICK⟩

A hundred golden ROMs never noticed, because all hundred are homebrew, and homebrew doesn't sit around waiting for H-blank. Chrono Trigger came back too, by the way — the picture you were dragging a slider over earlier only exists because of this fix.

Four lines → F-Zero boots

Launch title. LoROM. No coprocessor. Never drew a frame.

100 goldens never noticed — all homebrew

Chrono Trigger came back too (the slider you dragged!)

Corroboration

The tool agrees with someone who never used it

Ranked across 76 cartridges, the most CPU-starved games in the library include Super R-Type and Contra III.

Those are two of the games Vitor Vilela actually chose to convert to the SA-1 — years ago, by playing them.

Kirby Super Star is the joke: it already has an SA-1, and still drops frames.

One last thing, because a measurement you can't check is just a number.

⟨CLICK⟩

Run the profiler across seventy-six cartridges and rank them. Near the top — the most CPU-starved games in the library — are Super R-Type and Contra III.

⟨CLICK⟩

Those are two of the games Vitor Vilela actually picked to convert to the SA-1 co-processor. He chose them years ago, by playing them and noticing they were slow. He has never heard of this tool. And it independently ranks his picks at the top.

⟨CLICK⟩

Kirby Super Star is my favourite result: it already has an SA-1 in the cartridge, and it still drops frames.

76 carts ranked

Super R-Type + Contra III near the top

= two games Vilela ACTUALLY converted, years ago, by playing them

Kirby: already has an SA-1, still drops frames

The through-line

"don't regress the renderer"→ a counter that must not move
"don't serialize derived state"→ a type that won't compile
"don't add a dependency"→ a linker assertion
"ship no shader that can't run"→ a bake that refuses
"never move a Console"→ documented three times

Turn every invariant into something a machine checks. It works — and it is not enough. The upside-down picture, the black shaders, the launch title that never booted: all of them survived every check, and every one of them was found by running the real thing and looking at it.

So, the through-line.

⟨CLICK⟩

The whole discipline of this project is turning a rule you'd otherwise write in a style guide into something a machine checks. Don't regress the renderer becomes a counter that must not move. Don't serialize derived state becomes a type that won't compile. Don't add a dependency becomes a linker assertion. Ship no shader that can't run becomes a bake step that refuses.

⟨CLICK⟩

And never move a Console becomes... a comment. Three comments. Because sometimes you can't.

⟨CLICK⟩

That discipline works. I'd do it all again. And it is not enough — and I think that's the honest ending.

⟨CLICK⟩

The picture was upside down for the entire life of the project. Two shaders render black. A launch title never booted. Every one of those passed every check I had. Every one of them was found the same way: by running the real thing, on real hardware, and looking at it.

⟨CLICK⟩

The tests tell you that you didn't break what you already understood. Only the real thing tells you what you never understood in the first place. Thank you.

Every invariant → a machine check

Except one: "never move a Console" → a comment

It works. It is NOT ENOUGH.

Upside down · black shaders · F-Zero — all passed every check

CLOSE: tests guard what you understood.
The real thing shows what you didn't.
